title: The Principle of Modular Independence
author: From: Software Architecture
content: A system is only as resilient as its weakest coupling. When one component fails, it should not cascade into the rest. Decouple your tasks to protect your progress.
action: Action: Isolate your current project’s most unstable element; create a 'buffer' so that its failure doesn't halt the entire system.

title: The Design for Disassembly
author: From: Industrial Engineering
content: Products should be as easy to take apart as they are to build. If your processes are locked in, you cannot iterate. Maintain the ability to pivot.
action: Action: Review a recurring process; could you redesign it to be more modular so you can replace parts of it later?

title: The Throughput Limitation
author: From: Queueing Theory
content: The speed of a system is defined by its bottleneck. Adding resources anywhere else is merely creating 'work in progress' inventory, not finished results.
action: Action: Stop adding 'new' features to your current project and spend the entire session clearing the primary bottleneck.

title: The Orthogonality Principle
author: From: Systems Design
content: Changes in one area should not require changes in another. Orthogonal design ensures that your systems are independent and maintainable.
action: Action: Check your workflow—does changing a 'data source' force you to re-do your entire 'formatting' process? Separate them.

title: The Feedback Lag
author: From: Control Theory
content: The time between an action and its result is a critical variable. When the lag is long, we tend to over-correct. Patience is the antidote to volatility.
action: Action: Identify a long-lag process; document the current input and commit to waiting for the result before making further changes.
